name: Velox
summary: Pedreira et al. (Meta, VLDB 2022): a composable C++ vectorised execution engine distributed as a *library*. Powers Spark Gluten (Velox-accelerated Spark), Presto/Prestissimo, and several internal Meta systems. Arrow-shaped vectors, adaptive operator specialisation, first-class complex-type support.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pedreira et al.'s **Velox** (Meta, VLDB 2022; open source 2022+) is a composable C++ vectorised execution engine — distributed as a *library* rather than as a complete database — designed to be reused across multiple data-processing engines. Where DuckDB and Photon ship as full systems with their own SQL frontends, Velox is the *backend half*: a pluggable execution engine that other systems integrate. As of 2026-04 it powers **Spark Gluten** (Velox-accelerated Spark via the Apache Gluten project), **Presto/Prestissimo** (the Velox-based Presto worker), and several internal Meta data-processing systems.

sources.label: Engineering at Meta — Velox open source announcement

sections.title: Architectural pillars
sections.content:
- **Vector primitives over Arrow-shaped columnar data** — every operator is a function over `RowVector`, `FlatVector`, `DictionaryVector`, `ConstantVector`, `LazyVector` types. The same vector representations Apache Arrow defines, so Velox interoperates trivially with the broader Arrow ecosystem.
- **Adaptive optimisation at runtime** — Velox specialises operator implementations based on observed data: if a column proves nullable-but-rarely-null, its kernel switches to a fast-path that handles nulls via branch prediction; if a join key proves to have low cardinality, the hash table morphs into a dictionary lookup.
- **First-class support for complex types** — nested structs, lists, maps, and decimals get full vectorised treatment, where many SQL engines fall back to row-by-row processing for non-scalar columns.
- **Separable from any one query language** — Velox accepts plans expressed in its own internal IR, and existing engines (Presto SQL, Spark SQL, Substrait) compile their query plans *to* that IR.

sections.title: Lesson — execution engine as library
sections.content: The lesson generalises: **a column-vectorised engine designed as a library, not a system, can amortise across multiple frontends** — the same execution-engine design pattern that LLVM established for compiler backends, applied to query execution. The cost is that Velox cannot make end-to-end planning decisions (it sees the plan after the frontend's planner is done); the benefit is that one execution-engine investment serves many SQL dialects and several distributed-execution architectures.

sources.label: Engineering at Meta — Velox open source announcement
